**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roaring as if the very heavens were tearing apart. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may be the last one I shall see. You know, I've kept this lighthouse for twenty years, and never once did I think I'd see the day when I might not make it through another night. But tonight, the sky is black as pitch, and the waves are rising like mountains. I’ve seen many storms in my time, but this one feels different—more fierce, more cruel. I pray to God that I may survive it, and more than that, that I may live to see you again.

I’ve not heard from you in five long years, Eleanor. I know you’ve been angry, and perhaps still are. I’ve not written to you, not because I don’t care, but because I feared the words would not be enough. I’ve tried to find you, to reach you, but the sea has kept us apart, just as it has kept us from being what we once were.

You were but a girl when you left, and now you are a woman, I suppose, though I can’t imagine what you’ve become. I have no idea where you are, or what you do, but I hope you are well. I hope the world has not treated you as harshly as the sea has treated me.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it may have taken a long time to reach you. I write to you now, as the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars with fury, as if the very earth itself is crying out. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that the silence has grown like a thorn in my side. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I feel the need to reach out, to tell you what I have not had the heart to say.

You were a child of light and laughter, and I have missed your presence more than words can convey. I have watched the years pass like the tide, slow and relentless, and I have been a solitary figure in this tower, with only the sea and the stars for company. Yet, I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have kept your drawings, your letters, the little trinkets you left behind—each one a memory, a piece of you.

I have always believed that the sea would bring me peace, but tonight, it seems to bring me only fear. The storm is unlike any I have seen, and I fear for my life. But I have lived through many storms, and I have always believed that the dawn would come. Perhaps that is the lesson I have learned—no matter how dark the night, the light will return.

My Dearest Eleanor,

  

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I have not penned a word to you in five long years, and I must confess, I have not been certain whether you would read it. But the wind howls like a beast in the night, and the sea roars with a voice that shakes the very bones of this old lighthouse. I fear the storm may take more than it gives, and so I write, not for the world, but for the soul.

  

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. You were sharp of tongue and sharp of wit, and though you might have been more forgiving, I must say I always admired your spirit. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door, your eyes like cold steel, and your voice like a blade. "You do not know me," you said, "and I do not know you." And so we parted, not as friends, but as two souls who had once been one, and had grown apart. I did not try to reach you, and I do not regret it. I was afraid to. Afraid that the words would not be enough, and that the silence would be a kinder gift.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of my quill trembling, for the wind howls like the wails of the damned, and the sea, that old and restless beast, roars with a fury I have never known. The lantern, which I have tended for thirty years, flickers with a feeble glow, as though the very light of the world is fading. I fear I may not see the dawn.

You, my daughter, have not heard from me in five long years. I do not know if I have the strength to write more, or if I shall live to see the letters you have sent me answered. I have turned my back on the world, and you have turned yours upon me. Yet, in this hour of darkness, I find myself thinking of you.

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I have been of late. Five years have passed since our last correspondence, and though the sea may have calmed, the silence between us has grown rougher with each passing tide. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of a heart that must be laid to rest—before the storm that is yet to come.

  

You were never a child who took kindly to the lighthouse, nor to the solitude that comes with it. You were always eager to leave, to run to the city, to chase the world that called to you. I watched you grow, my Eleanor, and I watched you slip away, one step at a time. I did not stop you. I did not beg you to stay. I thought, perhaps, that you would return of your own accord, that the sea, in time, would call you back. But it has not. And I have not.  

  

I have not seen the city in years. I have not heard your voice in the distance, nor seen your face in the crowd. I have not seen your letters, save for the few that came—few, and filled with words that did not quite match the silence between us. It has been five long years since the last time I set eyes upon you, and I find myself now, on this storm-lashed night, with the wind howling like a beast in the very bones of the earth. The sea, that old, unrelenting thing, has been restless all day, and I fear the worst may yet come. I have not spoken to you in this time, nor have I written, though I have often thought of you. I have not seen you since the day you left, with your mother, in the spring of 1857. I remember the way you stood at the door, your eyes filled with tears, your hands clasped tightly in front of you, as if to keep the world from falling apart. You were barely sixteen, and yet you had the strength of a woman. I did not understand you then, nor did I understand what it was you were trying to say.

You were not the first to leave me, nor will you be the last. I have seen many children grow up, leave home, and become something more than they were. You were not wrong to go. I was not wrong to let you go. But I have not been the same since.

**The world has turned,**

**And I, with it,**

**Yet still, I write,**

**I write not for the world,**
